[Lucas Oil Stadium](/wiki/Lucas_Oil_Stadium "Lucas Oil Stadium"), [Indianapolis, Indiana](/wiki/Indianapolis,_Indiana "Indianapolis, Indiana")

was the 92nd [regular season](/wiki/Regular_season_(NFL) "Regular season (NFL)") of the [National Football League](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League") (NFL) and the 46th of the [Super Bowl](/wiki/Super_Bowl "Super Bowl") era. It began on Thursday, September 8, 2011, with the [Super Bowl XLV](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XLV "Super Bowl XLV") champion [Green Bay Packers](/wiki/2011_Green_Bay_Packers_season "2011 Green Bay Packers season") defeating the [Super Bowl XLIV](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XLIV "Super Bowl XLIV") champion [New Orleans Saints](/wiki/2011_New_Orleans_Saints_season "2011 New Orleans Saints season") at [Lambeau Field](/wiki/Lambeau_Field "Lambeau Field") and ended with [Super Bowl XLVI](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XLVI "Super Bowl XLVI"), the league's championship game, on February 5, 2012, at [Lucas Oil Stadium](/wiki/Lucas_Oil_Stadium "Lucas Oil Stadium") in [Indianapolis](/wiki/Indianapolis "Indianapolis") where the [New York Giants](/wiki/2011_New_York_Giants_season "2011 New York Giants season") defeated the [New England Patriots](/wiki/2011_New_England_Patriots_season "2011 New England Patriots season") 21–17.

The **2011 NFL season** was the 92nd [regular season](/wiki/Regular_season_(NFL) "Regular season (NFL)") of the [National Football League](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League") (NFL) and the 46th of the [Super Bowl](/wiki/Super_Bowl "Super Bowl") era. It began on Thursday, September 8, 2011, with the [Super Bowl XLV](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XLV "Super Bowl XLV") champion [Green Bay Packers](/wiki/2011_Green_Bay_Packers_season "2011 Green Bay Packers season") defeating the [Super Bowl XLIV](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XLIV "Super Bowl XLIV") champion [New Orleans Saints](/wiki/2011_New_Orleans_Saints_season "2011 New Orleans Saints season") at [Lambeau Field](/wiki/Lambeau_Field "Lambeau Field") and ended with [Super Bowl XLVI](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XLVI "Super Bowl XLVI"), the league's championship game, on February 5, 2012, at [Lucas Oil Stadium](/wiki/Lucas_Oil_Stadium "Lucas Oil Stadium") in [Indianapolis](/wiki/Indianapolis "Indianapolis") where the [New York Giants](/wiki/2011_New_York_Giants_season "2011 New York Giants season") defeated the [New England Patriots](/wiki/2011_New_England_Patriots_season "2011 New England Patriots season") 21–17.

Due to a labor dispute between league owners and players, a [lockout](/wiki/2011_NFL_lockout "2011 NFL lockout") began on March 11 and ended on July 25, lasting 130 days. Although it initially threatened to postpone or cancel the season, the only game that was canceled was the August 7 [Pro Football Hall of Fame Game](/wiki/Pro_Football_Hall_of_Fame_Game "Pro Football Hall of Fame Game").

The 2011 season saw an unprecedented amount of passing offense: Three of the nine highest passing yardage totals of all time were established: No. 2 [Drew Brees](/wiki/Drew_Brees "Drew Brees") (5,476), No. 3 [Tom Brady](/wiki/Tom_Brady "Tom Brady") (5,235), and No. 9 [Matthew Stafford](/wiki/Matthew_Stafford "Matthew Stafford") (5,038); [Eli Manning](/wiki/Eli_Manning "Eli Manning") threw for 4,933 yards, which places him 14th all time.[[1]](#cite_note-1) It also saw Green Bay Packers quarterback [Aaron Rodgers](/wiki/Aaron_Rodgers "Aaron Rodgers") establish the all-time single-season best quarterback rating of 122.5.[[2]](#cite_note-2) Further cementing the modern NFL's reputation as a "passing league"[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5) was that, for the second consecutive year, the league overall set a record for most average passing yards per team per game, with 229.7, breaking 2010's record by more than eight yards per game.[[6]](#cite_note-6) (For comparison, the league-wide average rushing yards total finished the 2011 season at 57th all-time.)

A subplot of the 2011 season was determining who would have the worst record, and therefore "earn" the right to the No. 1 overall pick in the [2012 draft](/wiki/2012_NFL_draft "2012 NFL draft"). [Stanford](/wiki/Stanford_Cardinal_football "Stanford Cardinal football") senior quarterback [Andrew Luck](/wiki/Andrew_Luck "Andrew Luck") was seen as the best quarterback prospect in years. Fans of some teams that started the season with numerous losses (notably the [Indianapolis Colts](/wiki/2011_Indianapolis_Colts_season "2011 Indianapolis Colts season")) were openly rooting for their teams to "Suck for Luck."[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8)

## Labor dispute

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2011_NFL_season&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Labor dispute")]

Main article: [2011 NFL lockout](/wiki/2011_NFL_lockout "2011 NFL lockout")

In May 2008 the owners decided to opt out of the 1993 arrangement and play the 2010 season without an agreement in place.[[9]](#cite_note-Opt_out-9) Some of the major points of contention included openness of owners' financial books, the rookie pay scale, a proposed 18 percent reduction in the players' share of revenues, forfeiture on bonus payments for players who fail to perform, players' health and retirement benefits, details of free agency, the cost and benefit of new stadiums, players' salaries, extending the regular season to 18 games, and the revenue-sharing structure.[[9]](#cite_note-Opt_out-9) By March 2011, the [National Football League Players Association](/wiki/National_Football_League_Players_Association "National Football League Players Association") (NFLPA) and the NFL had not yet come to terms on a new [collective bargaining agreement](/wiki/National_Football_League_collective_bargaining_agreement "National Football League collective bargaining agreement") (CBA), thus failing to resolve the labor dispute. Accordingly, the NFLPA informed the league and the clubs that the players had voted to have the NFLPA renounce its bargaining rights.[[10]](#cite_note-FoxSports-10) After the renunciation of collective bargaining rights, quarterbacks [Tom Brady](/wiki/Tom_Brady "Tom Brady"), [Peyton Manning](/wiki/Peyton_Manning "Peyton Manning"), and [Drew Brees](/wiki/Drew_Brees "Drew Brees") joined seven other NFL players and filed an antitrust suit to enjoin the lockout.[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[10]](#cite_note-FoxSports-10)[[12]](#cite_note-12)

Following the settlement of the *Brady et al.* v. *NFL* antitrust suit on July 25, 2011, a majority of players signed union authorization cards approving the NFL Players Association to act as their exclusive collective bargaining representative.[[13]](#cite_note-13) The NFL officially recognized the NFLPA's status as the players' collective bargaining representative on July 30.[[14]](#cite_note-14) The NFL and NFLPA proceeded to negotiate terms for a new collective bargaining agreement, and the agreement became effective after ratification by the players on August 4.[[15]](#cite_note-15) The new collective bargaining agreement ran through 2021.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

## NFL draft

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2011_NFL_season&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: NFL draft")]

The [2011 NFL draft](/wiki/2011_NFL_draft "2011 NFL draft") was held from April 28 to 30, 2011 at [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City")'s [Radio City Music Hall](/wiki/Radio_City_Music_Hall "Radio City Music Hall"). With the first pick, the [Carolina Panthers](/wiki/Carolina_Panthers "Carolina Panthers") selected quarterback [Cam Newton](/wiki/Cam_Newton "Cam Newton") from [Auburn](/wiki/Auburn_University "Auburn University").

## Rule changes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2011_NFL_season&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Rule changes")]

The following are rule changes that were passed at the league's annual owners meeting in March. These rule changes went into effect once the labor dispute was resolved.

* Changes were made regarding kickoffs to limit injuries. First, kickoffs will be moved from the 30 to the 35-yard line, repealing a [1994](/wiki/1994_NFL_season "1994 NFL season") rule change, restoring the [1974](/wiki/1974_NFL_season "1974 NFL season") rule. In addition, players on the kickoff coverage team cannot line up more than 5 yards behind the kickoff line, minimizing running starts and thus reducing the speed of collisions.[[29]](#cite_note-NFL_ruleChanges-29) Other changes were also proposed, but a number of players and coaches expressed concern they would actually significantly reduce, if not eliminate, the number of kickoff returns.[[30]](#cite_note-LATimes_ruleChanges-30)[[31]](#cite_note-BGlobe_ruleChanges-31) Proposals that would have brought touchbacks out to the 25 instead of the 20, and eliminated all wedge blocks were not adopted.[[29]](#cite_note-NFL_ruleChanges-29) Despite this rule, the [Bears](/wiki/Chicago_Bears "Chicago Bears") kicked off from the 30-yard line twice[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*][*[why?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] in their preseason game against the [Bills](/wiki/Buffalo_Bills "Buffalo Bills").
* All replay reviews of scoring plays during the entire game can now be initiated by the replay booth official. Coaches will no longer have to use one of their challenges if a scoring play occurs outside of the two-minute warning.[[29]](#cite_note-NFL_ruleChanges-29)[[30]](#cite_note-LATimes_ruleChanges-30) Because the play is now "unchallengeable" by coaches, attempting to do so will result in a 15-yard unsportsmanlike conduct penalty, which several coaches were flagged for during the season.
* Nicknamed the "[Boise State](/wiki/Boise_State_Broncos_football "Boise State Broncos football") Rule", all playing fields must remain green, and not be in another color like the blue turf at Boise State's [Bronco Stadium](/wiki/Bronco_Stadium "Bronco Stadium"), unless approval is granted by the league. This was passed in response to a few sponsors who requested to change the colors in a few stadiums.[[32]](#cite_note-NYTimes_ruleChanges-32)

The following rule changes were adopted at the NFL Owners' Meeting on May 24, 2011:

* Hits to the head of a passer-by an opponent's hands, arms or other parts of the body will not be fouls unless they are forcible blows, modifying the existing rule that any contact to a passer's head, regardless of the reason, is penalized as a personal foul (15 yards).
* Players will be prohibited from "launching" (leaving both feet prior to contact to spring forward and upward into an opponent or using any part of the helmet to initiate forcible contact against any part of the opponent's body) to level a defenseless player, as well as "forcibly hitting the neck or head area with the helmet, facemask, forearm or shoulder regardless of whether the defensive player also uses his arms to tackle the defenseless player by encircling or grasping him.", and lowering the head and make forcible contact with the top/crown or forehead/"hairline" parts of the helmet against any part of the defenseless player's body. Offenders will be penalised 15 yards for unnecessary roughness plus risking immediate disqualification if the contact is deemed flagrant.

A "defenseless player" is defined as a:

* Player in the act of or just after throwing a pass.
* Receiver attempting to catch a pass or one who has not completed a catch and hasn't had time to protect himself or hasn't clearly become a runner. If the receiver/runner is capable of avoiding or warding off the impending contact of an opponent, he is no longer a defenseless player.
* Runner whose forward progress has been stopped and is already in the grasp of a tackler.
* Kickoff or punt returner attempting to field a kick in the air.
* Player on the ground at the end of a play.
* Kicker/punter during the kick or return.
* Quarterback any time after a change of possession (i.e. turnover).
* Player who receives a "blindside" block when the blocker is moving toward his own end-line and approaches the opponent from behind or the side.[[33]](#cite_note-FoxSports_ruleChanges-33)

The league has instructed game officials to "err on the side of caution" when calling such personal foul penalties, and that they will not be downgraded if they make a mistake so that they will not hesitate on making these kinds of calls.[[34]](#cite_note-34)

### Game-day testing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2011_NFL_season&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Game-day testing")]

* Game-day testing for performance-enhancing drugs. The NFL is adding game-day testing for performance-enhancing substances but not recreational drugs this season under the new collective bargaining agreement.[[35]](#cite_note-35)

## Schedule

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2011_NFL_season&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Schedule")]

The preseason schedule was released April 12, 2011. The Hall of Fame Game, had it been played, would have featured the [Chicago Bears](/wiki/2011_Chicago_Bears_season "2011 Chicago Bears season") against the [St. Louis Rams](/wiki/2011_St._Louis_Rams_season "2011 St. Louis Rams season") in only the second time since 1971 that the game would have featured two teams from the same conference.[[36]](#cite_note-36) Instead, the preseason began with the [San Diego Chargers](/wiki/2011_San_Diego_Chargers_season "2011 San Diego Chargers season") hosting the [Seattle Seahawks](/wiki/2011_Seattle_Seahawks_season "2011 Seattle Seahawks season") on August 11; the remainder of the preseason and all other games was played as originally scheduled (with the exception of the preseason [New York Jets](/wiki/2011_New_York_Jets_season "2011 New York Jets season")-[New York Giants](/wiki/2011_New_York_Giants_season "2011 New York Giants season") game, which was postponed two days due to [Hurricane Irene](/wiki/Hurricane_Irene "Hurricane Irene")).

The 2011 season began on Thursday, September 8, 2011, at [Lambeau Field](/wiki/Lambeau_Field "Lambeau Field"), with the [Super Bowl XLV](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XLV "Super Bowl XLV") champion [Green Bay Packers](/wiki/2011_Green_Bay_Packers_season "2011 Green Bay Packers season") hosting the [New Orleans Saints](/wiki/2011_New_Orleans_Saints_season "2011 New Orleans Saints season") in the [kickoff game](/wiki/National_Football_League_Kickoff_game "National Football League Kickoff game"); the last regular season games were held on Sunday, January 1, 2012. The playoffs started on Saturday, January 7, 2012, and ended with [Super Bowl XLVI](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XLVI "Super Bowl XLVI"), the league's championship game, on February 5, 2012, at [Lucas Oil Stadium](/wiki/Lucas_Oil_Stadium "Lucas Oil Stadium") in [Indianapolis](/wiki/Indianapolis "Indianapolis").

Under the NFL's [scheduling formula](/wiki/NFL_scheduling_formula "NFL scheduling formula"), intraconference and interconference matchups were:

**Intraconference**

* [AFC East](/wiki/AFC_East "AFC East") vs. [AFC West](/wiki/AFC_West "AFC West")
* [AFC North](/wiki/AFC_North "AFC North") vs. [AFC South](/wiki/AFC_South "AFC South")
* [NFC East](/wiki/NFC_East "NFC East") vs. [NFC West](/wiki/NFC_West "NFC West")
* [NFC North](/wiki/NFC_North "NFC North") vs. [NFC South](/wiki/NFC_South "NFC South")

**Interconference**

* [AFC East](/wiki/AFC_East "AFC East") vs. [NFC East](/wiki/NFC_East "NFC East")
* [AFC West](/wiki/AFC_West "AFC West") vs. [NFC North](/wiki/NFC_North "NFC North")
* [AFC North](/wiki/AFC_North "AFC North") vs. [NFC West](/wiki/NFC_West "NFC West")
* [AFC South](/wiki/AFC_South "AFC South") vs. [NFC South](/wiki/NFC_South "NFC South")

When the league was arranging the schedule in spring 2011, it added some cushion in case the labor dispute lasted into September and the planned start of the regular season. For example, every contest in Week 3 had teams which shared the same bye week later in the season, which would have allowed these games to be made up on what were originally the teams' byes. Weeks 2 and 4 were set up so that there were neither any divisional rivalry games nor teams on bye in those weeks, and every team with a home game in Week 2 was on the road in Week 4 and vice versa. This would have kept the season as fair as possible if those games had to be canceled.[[37]](#cite_note-ESPN_Schefter20100421-37) These scheduling changes, along with eliminating the week off before the Super Bowl and moving the Super Bowl back a week, would have allowed the NFL to play a 14-game schedule beginning in mid-October while still having the Super Bowl in mid-February, allowing for as many as the first five weeks to be lost without having to redo the schedule (moving week 3 games into the bye weeks, canceling weeks 2 and 4, and moving weeks 1 and 5 to the end of the season, while postponing the start of the playoffs two weeks, and moving the Super Bowl back a week and having no week off between the conference championship games and Super Bowl).

This season's [International Series game](/wiki/NFL_International_Series "NFL International Series") featured the [Chicago Bears](/wiki/2011_Chicago_Bears_season "2011 Chicago Bears season") and the [Tampa Bay Buccaneers](/wiki/2011_Tampa_Bay_Buccaneers_season "2011 Tampa Bay Buccaneers season") at [Wembley Stadium](/wiki/Wembley_Stadium "Wembley Stadium") in London on October 23, with the Buccaneers serving as the home team.[[38]](#cite_note-38) The Bears won 24–18.[[39]](#cite_note-39) It marked the Bears' second game played outside the United States in as many years, as they were a part of the [Bills Toronto Series](/wiki/Bills_Toronto_Series "Bills Toronto Series") in 2010. The Buccaneers previously appeared in the International Series in 2009. One week later on October 30, the [Buffalo Bills](/wiki/2011_Buffalo_Bills_season "2011 Buffalo Bills season") defeated the [Washington Redskins](/wiki/2011_Washington_Redskins_season "2011 Washington Redskins season") in the Bills' annual game at [Rogers Centre](/wiki/Rogers_Centre "Rogers Centre") in [Toronto](/wiki/Toronto "Toronto") by a score of 23–0. Although this was within the bounds of the [2011 CFL season](/wiki/2011_CFL_season "2011 CFL season"), neither of the two Southern Ontario CFL teams was playing on the same day, and both played away games that weekend. The 2011–12 season also marked the 20th anniversary of the Bills and Redskins meeting in [Super Bowl XXVI](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XXVI "Super Bowl XXVI").

The [Detroit Lions](/wiki/2011_Detroit_Lions_season "2011 Detroit Lions season") hosted their first *[Monday Night Football](/wiki/Monday_Night_Football "Monday Night Football")* game since [2001](/wiki/2001_Detroit_Lions_season "2001 Detroit Lions season"), when they faced the [Chicago Bears](/wiki/2011_Chicago_Bears_season "2011 Chicago Bears season") on [Columbus Day](/wiki/Columbus_Day "Columbus Day")/[Canadian Thanksgiving](/wiki/Canadian_Thanksgiving "Canadian Thanksgiving") (the [Detroit-Windsor](/wiki/Detroit-Windsor "Detroit-Windsor") market straddles the U.S.–Canada border).[[40]](#cite_note-40) The Lions defeated the Bears 24–13 for the team's fifth straight win, the most Lions wins to start a season since the team's glory years in the 1950s, continuing a streak that has been seen as a pleasant surprise for Lions fans, after over a decade of mediocrity.[[41]](#cite_note-41)

The 2011 [Thanksgiving Day slate](/wiki/NFL_on_Thanksgiving_Day "NFL on Thanksgiving Day") featured the Super Bowl Champion [Green Bay Packers](/wiki/2011_Green_Bay_Packers_season "2011 Green Bay Packers season") winning 27–15 on the road against the [Detroit Lions](/wiki/2011_Detroit_Lions_season "2011 Detroit Lions season") and the [Dallas Cowboys](/wiki/2011_Dallas_Cowboys_season "2011 Dallas Cowboys season") coming back to defeat the [Miami Dolphins](/wiki/2011_Miami_Dolphins_season "2011 Miami Dolphins season") 20–19 at home. The Thanksgiving nightcap on the [NFL Network](/wiki/NFL_Network "NFL Network") showed the [Baltimore Ravens](/wiki/2011_Baltimore_Ravens_season "2011 Baltimore Ravens season") defeating the [San Francisco 49ers](/wiki/2011_San_Francisco_49ers_season "2011 San Francisco 49ers season") 16–6 at home; this was the first Thanksgiving game for the 49ers since 1972, the first ever for the Ravens, and a game that put first-year 49ers head coach [Jim Harbaugh](/wiki/Jim_Harbaugh "Jim Harbaugh") against his brother, Ravens head coach [John Harbaugh](/wiki/John_Harbaugh "John Harbaugh").

Christmas Day fell on Sunday. The TV contracts stated that the majority of afternoon games would be played on Christmas Eve (Saturday) and only one game was held over for Sunday night. The [Green Bay Packers](/wiki/2011_Green_Bay_Packers_season "2011 Green Bay Packers season") defeated the [Chicago Bears](/wiki/2011_Chicago_Bears_season "2011 Chicago Bears season"), 35–21, on [Christmas evening](/wiki/National_Football_League_Christmas_games "National Football League Christmas games") on [NBC](/wiki/NBC "NBC").

New Year's Day 2012 consequently also fell on a Sunday, and the NFL played its entire Week 17 schedule that day. The major college bowl games usually played on New Year's Day, as well as the [NHL Winter Classic](/wiki/2012_NHL_Winter_Classic "2012 NHL Winter Classic"), were instead played on Monday, January 2. For the second straight year, Week 17 only featured divisional match-ups.

The [New York Giants](/wiki/2011_New_York_Giants_season "2011 New York Giants season") visited the [Washington Redskins](/wiki/2011_Washington_Redskins_season "2011 Washington Redskins season") on September 11, 2011, the first Sunday of the regular season, to commemorate the tenth anniversary of the [September 11 attacks](/wiki/September_11_attacks "September 11 attacks") in which Washington, D.C. and New York City were both targeted, as well as the first such anniversary since the [killing of](/wiki/Death_of_Osama_bin_Laden "Death of Osama bin Laden") Osama bin Laden in May.[[42]](#cite_note-42) Due to the proximity of [Baltimore](/wiki/Baltimore "Baltimore") with Washington as well as the proximity of [Pittsburgh](/wiki/Pittsburgh "Pittsburgh") with the [site](/wiki/Flight_93_Memorial "Flight 93 Memorial") where [United Airlines Flight 93](/wiki/United_Airlines_Flight_93 "United Airlines Flight 93") crashed, the [Pittsburgh Steelers](/wiki/2011_Pittsburgh_Steelers_season "2011 Pittsburgh Steelers season") visited the [archrival](/wiki/Steelers%E2%80%93Ravens_rivalry "Steelers–Ravens rivalry") [Baltimore Ravens](/wiki/2011_Baltimore_Ravens_season "2011 Baltimore Ravens season") at [M&T Bank Stadium](/wiki/M%26T_Bank_Stadium "M&T Bank Stadium") in Baltimore. It marked the first time the two teams played in a season-opening game since [2003](/wiki/2003_NFL_season "2003 NFL season"), as their heated rivalry usually prompts their games to be scheduled later in the season. There had been some speculation that the Giants and their same-city rival, the [New York Jets](/wiki/2011_New_York_Jets_season "2011 New York Jets season"), could have played each other that day since the two were scheduled to play each other in 2011; the Jets were the designated home team at [MetLife Stadium](/wiki/MetLife_Stadium "MetLife Stadium") in the matchup which had been predetermined due to the NFL's scheduling formula.[[43]](#cite_note-43) However, the Jets instead hosted the [Dallas Cowboys](/wiki/2011_Dallas_Cowboys_season "2011 Dallas Cowboys season").[[44]](#cite_note-44)

### Scheduling changes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2011_NFL_season&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Scheduling changes")]

The following regular-season games were moved by way of [flexible scheduling](/wiki/NFL_on_Television#Flexible_scheduling "NFL on Television"), severe weather, or for other reasons:

* **Week 10**: The [Detroit](/wiki/2011_Detroit_Lions_season "2011 Detroit Lions season")–[Chicago](/wiki/2011_Chicago_Bears_season "2011 Chicago Bears season") game was moved from 1:00 pm [EST](/wiki/North_American_Eastern_Time_Zone "North American Eastern Time Zone") to 4:15 pm EST.[[45]](#cite_note-45)
* **Week 11**: The [Tennessee](/wiki/2011_Tennessee_Titans_season "2011 Tennessee Titans season")–[Atlanta](/wiki/2011_Atlanta_Falcons_season "2011 Atlanta Falcons season") game was moved from 1:00 pm EST to 4:15 pm EST.[[46]](#cite_note-TitansFalconsColtsPatriots-46)
* **Week 13**: The [Indianapolis](/wiki/2011_Indianapolis_Colts_season "2011 Indianapolis Colts season")–[New England](/wiki/2011_New_England_Patriots_season "2011 New England Patriots season") game was moved from the 8:20 pm EST time slot on [NBC Sunday Night Football](/wiki/NBC_Sunday_Night_Football "NBC Sunday Night Football") to 1:00 pm EST on [CBS](/wiki/NFL_on_CBS "NFL on CBS"). The Detroit–[New Orleans](/wiki/2011_New_Orleans_Saints_season "2011 New Orleans Saints season") game, originally scheduled at 1:00 pm EST on [Fox](/wiki/NFL_on_Fox "NFL on Fox"), was flexed into the 8:20 pm slot on NBC, in place of the originally-scheduled Colts–Patriots game. The [Baltimore](/wiki/2011_Baltimore_Ravens_season "2011 Baltimore Ravens season")–[Cleveland](/wiki/2011_Cleveland_Browns_season "2011 Cleveland Browns season") game was changed from 1:00 pm EST to 4:05 pm EST. The [Denver](/wiki/2011_Denver_Broncos_season "2011 Denver Broncos season")–[Minnesota](/wiki/2011_Minnesota_Vikings_season "2011 Minnesota Vikings season") game was changed from 4:05 pm EST to 1:00 pm EST, and aired on Fox instead of CBS because Fox had only two games in the early time slot. This was the first time that the league moved an interconference telecast to the home team's Sunday afternoon regional broadcaster.[[46]](#cite_note-TitansFalconsColtsPatriots-46)[[47]](#cite_note-47)
* **Week 14**: The [Oakland](/wiki/2011_Oakland_Raiders_season "2011 Oakland Raiders season")–[Green Bay](/wiki/2011_Green_Bay_Packers_season "2011 Green Bay Packers season") game was moved from 1:00 pm EST to 4:15 pm EST.[[48]](#cite_note-48)
* **Week 17**: By way of flexible scheduling, the following games were moved due to playoff implications during the final week of the regular season: The [Dallas](/wiki/2011_Dallas_Cowboys_season "2011 Dallas Cowboys season")–[New York Giants](/wiki/2011_New_York_Giants_season "2011 New York Giants season") game, originally scheduled at 1:00 pm EST on Fox, was selected as the final NBC Sunday Night Football game, which decided the [NFC East](/wiki/NFC_East "NFC East") division champion. ## Records and milestones

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2011_NFL_season&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Records and milestones")]

* Most points in the [Kickoff Game](/wiki/National_Football_League_Kickoff_game "National Football League Kickoff game"), single team: 42, [Green Bay](/wiki/Green_Bay_Packers "Green Bay Packers") (vs. [New Orleans](/wiki/New_Orleans_Saints "New Orleans Saints"), September 8, 2011)
* Most points in the [Kickoff Game](/wiki/National_Football_League_Kickoff_game "National Football League Kickoff game"), total: 76, [Green Bay](/wiki/Green_Bay_Packers "Green Bay Packers") (42) and [New Orleans](/wiki/New_Orleans_Saints "New Orleans Saints") (34) – September 8, 2011
* Longest kick return (tie): 108 yards, [Randall Cobb](/wiki/Randall_Cobb_(American_football) "Randall Cobb (American football)") ([Green Bay](/wiki/Green_Bay_Packers "Green Bay Packers") vs. [New Orleans](/wiki/New_Orleans_Saints "New Orleans Saints") – September 8, 2011)
* Longest field goal (tie): 63 yards, [Sebastian Janikowski](/wiki/Sebastian_Janikowski "Sebastian Janikowski") ([Oakland](/wiki/Oakland_Raiders "Oakland Raiders") vs. [Denver](/wiki/Denver_Broncos "Denver Broncos") – September 12, 2011)[[51]](#cite_note-mmqb919-64)
* Most combined passing yards in a single game, broken twice:
  + 933, [Tom Brady](/wiki/Tom_Brady "Tom Brady") ([New England](/wiki/New_England_Patriots "New England Patriots"), 517) and [Chad Henne](/wiki/Chad_Henne "Chad Henne") ([Miami](/wiki/Miami_Dolphins "Miami Dolphins"), 416) – September 12, 2011[[51]](#cite_note-mmqb919-64)
  + 1,000, [Matthew Stafford](/wiki/Matthew_Stafford "Matthew Stafford") ([Detroit](/wiki/Detroit_Lions "Detroit Lions"), 520) and [Matt Flynn](/wiki/Matt_Flynn_(American_football) "Matt Flynn (American football)") ([Green Bay](/wiki/Green_Bay_Packers "Green Bay Packers"), 480) – January 1, 2012
* Most yards thrown by a rookie quarterback in his first game: 422, [Cam Newton](/wiki/Cam_Newton "Cam Newton") ([Carolina](/wiki/Carolina_Panthers "Carolina Panthers") vs. [Arizona](/wiki/Arizona_Cardinals "Arizona Cardinals"))[[52]](#cite_note-65)
* Most passing yards, rookie, season: 4,051, Cam Newton, Carolina
* Most yards thrown by a quarterback, first two games of the season, broken twice:
  + 854 yards, Cam Newton (September 18, 2011), [Carolina](/wiki/Carolina_Panthers "Carolina Panthers"), stands as record for a rookie[[53]](#cite_note-Forbes432-66)
  + 940 yards, Tom Brady (September 18, 2011), New England Patriots[[53]](#cite_note-Forbes432-66)
* Most consecutive second-half drives to end in touchdowns: 5, [Buffalo](/wiki/Buffalo_Bills "Buffalo Bills") (vs. [Oakland](/wiki/Oakland_Raiders "Oakland Raiders"), September 18, 2011)[[54]](#cite_note-67)
* Largest point margin prior to a successful comeback in consecutive games, modern era, broken twice:
  + 18, [Buffalo](/wiki/Buffalo_Bills "Buffalo Bills") (18 vs. [Oakland](/wiki/Oakland_Raiders "Oakland Raiders"), 21 vs. [New England](/wiki/New_England_Patriots "New England Patriots"))[[55]](#cite_note-68)
  + 20, [Detroit](/wiki/Detroit_Lions "Detroit Lions") (20 vs. [Minnesota](/wiki/Minnesota_Vikings "Minnesota Vikings"), 24 vs. [Dallas](/wiki/Dallas_Cowboys "Dallas Cowboys"))
* Most field goals of 50 or more yards, single game (tied twice):
  + 3, [Sebastian Janikowski](/wiki/Sebastian_Janikowski "Sebastian Janikowski"), Oakland (54, 55, and 50; vs. [Houston](/wiki/Houston_Texans "Houston Texans"), October 9, 2011)[[56]](#cite_note-69)
  + 3, [Josh Scobee](/wiki/Josh_Scobee "Josh Scobee"), Jacksonville (54, 54, and 51; vs. [Baltimore](/wiki/Baltimore_Ravens "Baltimore Ravens"), October 24, 2011)
* Highest net punting average for a season: 43.99 yards, [Andy Lee](/wiki/Andy_Lee_(American_football) "Andy Lee (American football)"), [San Francisco](/wiki/San_Francisco_49ers "San Francisco 49ers")[[57]](#cite_note-csn49er_records-70)
* Longest game-winning punt return touchdown in overtime: 99 yards, [Patrick Peterson](/wiki/Patrick_Peterson "Patrick Peterson"), Arizona (vs. St. Louis, November 6, 2011)[[58]](#cite_note-71)
* Most punt returns in a season for touchdown (tied): 4, Patrick Peterson, Arizona
* Most punt return yards by a rookie in a season: 699, Patrick Peterson, Arizona
* Most field goals in a season: 44, [David Akers](/wiki/David_Akers "David Akers"), San Francisco
* Most points in a season without a touchdown: 166, David Akers, San Francisco[[57]](#cite_note-csn49er_records-70)
* Most rushing touchdowns by a quarterback in a season: 14, Cam Newton, Carolina
* Most passing yards in a season: 5,476, [Drew Brees](/wiki/Drew_Brees "Drew Brees"), New Orleans.[[59]](#cite_note-72)
  + [Tom Brady](/wiki/Tom_Brady "Tom Brady"), New England (5,235) and [Matthew Stafford](/wiki/Matthew_Stafford "Matthew Stafford"), Detroit (5,038) also passed for more than 5,000 yards marking the 4th and 5th times an individual has reached that milestone in NFL history, and the first time more than one person has done it in a single season.
* Fewest turnovers in a season (tied): 10, San Francisco[[57]](#cite_note-csn49er_records-70)
* The 2011 [Saints](/wiki/New_Orleans_Saints "New Orleans Saints") broke many offensive records on January 1, 2012:[[60]](#cite_note-73)
  + Most net yardage of offense in a season: 7,474
  + Most net yards passing: 5,347
  + Most completions: 472
  + Highest completion percentage (team) for the season: 71.3
  + Fewest fumbles in a season: 6
  + Most first downs for the season: 416
  + Most passing first downs in a season: 280
  + Most kick-offs resulting in a touchback, season: 62
  + Highest third down conversion percentage: 57.9%
* The 2011 [Raiders](/wiki/Oakland_Raiders "Oakland Raiders") also broke a few records:
  + Most penalties, season: 163
  + Most yards penalized, season: 1,358
* Most all purpose yards in a season: 2,696, [Darren Sproles](/wiki/Darren_Sproles "Darren Sproles"), New Orleans
* Most receiving yards by a tight end in a season, broken twice:
  + 1310, ([Jimmy Graham](/wiki/Jimmy_Graham "Jimmy Graham"), New Orleans vs. [Carolina](/wiki/Carolina_Panthers "Carolina Panthers"))
  + 1327, ([Rob Gronkowski](/wiki/Rob_Gronkowski "Rob Gronkowski"), New England vs. [Buffalo](/wiki/Buffalo_Bills "Buffalo Bills"))
* Most games, 300+ yards passing, season: 13, Drew Brees, New Orleans
* Most consecutive 300+ yards passing games: 7, Drew Brees, New Orleans
* Punt return touchdowns, career: 12, Devin Hester, Chicago
* Most consecutive games, 100+ passer rating, season: 12, Aaron Rodgers, Green Bay
* Highest passer rating, season: 122.5, Aaron Rodgers, Green Bay
* Most field goals of 50 or more yards, season, all teams: 90
* Highest field goals of 50 or more yards percentage, season, all teams: 63.8
* [Highest completion percentage (individual), season](/wiki/List_of_National_Football_League_annual_pass_completion_percentage_leaders "List of National Football League annual pass completion percentage leaders"): 71.2, Drew Brees, New Orleans
* Longest pass completion (tied twice):
  + 99, Tom Brady, New England (vs. Miami, September 12, 2011)
  + 99, Eli Manning, New York Giants (vs. New York Jets, December 24, 2011)
* Most consecutive games, 2+ touchdown passes (tied): 13, Aaron Rodgers, Green Bay
* Most times finished in the first place: 23, [New York Giants](/wiki/New_York_Giants "New York Giants")

### Playoff records & milestones

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2011_NFL_season&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Playoff records & milestones")]

* Most offensive yards in a single playoff game: 627, New Orleans (vs. Detroit, Wild Card January 7, 2012)
* First quarterback to reach 400+ yards in two consecutive postseason games: Drew Brees, New Orleans (First time: 2010 vs. Seattle; 2nd time: 2011 vs. Detroit – both Wild Card games)

  + Shortest time of a drive in regular and postseason overtime: 11 seconds
  + Quickest win in overtime: 11 seconds
* Most playoff appearances: 31, [New York Giants](/wiki/New_York_Giants "New York Giants")
* Most completions to start a super bowl: 9, [Eli Manning](/wiki/Eli_Manning "Eli Manning")
* Most passing yards in a single postseason: 1,219, [Eli Manning](/wiki/Eli_Manning "Eli Manning")
* Most touchdown passes in a single playoff game (tie): 6, [Tom Brady](/wiki/Tom_Brady "Tom Brady"), New England
* Most league championship game appearances: 19, [New York Giants](/wiki/New_York_Giants "New York Giants")
* Most Super Bowls Started as QB (tie): 5, [Tom Brady](/wiki/Tom_Brady "Tom Brady")
* Record for most yards per completion (31.6) in an NFL playoff game [Tim Tebow](/wiki/Tim_Tebow "Tim Tebow")
* 3rd player in NFL playoff history to pass for 300 yards, and rush for 50 yards. [Tim Tebow](/wiki/Tim_Tebow "Tim Tebow")
* Most Super Bowls lost (tie): 4, [New England Patriots](/wiki/New_England_Patriots "New England Patriots")
* Most playoff games won starting QB (tie): 16, [Tom Brady](/wiki/Tom_Brady "Tom Brady")

### Regular-season awards

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2011_NFL_season&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Regular-season awards")]

Further information: [1st NFL Honors](/wiki/1st_NFL_Honors "1st NFL Honors")

For the first time, the league held the [NFL Honors](/wiki/NFL_Honors "NFL Honors"), an awards show to salute the best players and plays for the season. The [1st NFL Honors](/wiki/1st_NFL_Honors "1st NFL Honors") was held at the [Murat Theatre](/wiki/Murat_Theatre "Murat Theatre") in [Indianapolis, Indiana](/wiki/Indianapolis,_Indiana "Indianapolis, Indiana") on February 4, 2012.[[119]](#cite_note-132)

## Head coach/front office changes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2011_NFL_season&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Head coach/front office changes")]

### Head coach

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2011_NFL_season&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Head coach")]

Offseason

The uncertain labor issues and the possibility of a lockout were speculated to have a minimizing effect on coaching changes prior to the 2011 season, with owners predicted to be more hesitant than usual to hire a high-price, high-profile head coach.[[122]](#cite_note-ESPN_Coaching-135) Nevertheless, eight coaches were fired either during or immediately after the [2010 NFL season](/wiki/2010_NFL_season "2010 NFL season"), compared to three in the year prior; only one of the new hires ([John Fox](/wiki/John_Fox_(American_football) "John Fox (American football)")) had ever been a head coach in the NFL prior to their hirings or promotions. However, [Leslie Frazier](/wiki/Leslie_Frazier "Leslie Frazier"), and [Jason Garrett](/wiki/Jason_Garrett "Jason Garrett") did get some experience as interim coaches during the 2010 season, with Garrett being successful in his debut season, going 5–3 in his tenure, improving the 1–7 Cowboys to a 6–10 season.

## Stadiums

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2011_NFL_season&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Stadiums")]

### Naming rights agreements

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2011_NFL_season&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Naming rights agreements")]

The following stadiums received new [naming rights](/wiki/Naming_rights "Naming rights"):

* April 27: The Oakland-Alameda County Coliseum, home of the [Oakland Raiders](/wiki/Oakland_Raiders "Oakland Raiders"), was renamed Overstock.com Coliseum, and later shortened to [O.co Coliseum](/wiki/O.co_Coliseum "O.co Coliseum"). The Raiders' home field has undergone several name changes in its history, including Network Associates Coliseum (1998–2004) and McAfee Coliseum (2004–2008).[[135]](#cite_note-148)
* June 20: Qwest Field, the home of the [Seattle Seahawks](/wiki/Seattle_Seahawks "Seattle Seahawks") since [2002](/wiki/2002_NFL_season "2002 NFL season"), was renamed [CenturyLink Field](/wiki/CenturyLink_Field "CenturyLink Field"). [Qwest](/wiki/Qwest "Qwest")'s naming rights to the Seahawks' home field was set to expire in [2014](/wiki/2014_NFL_season "2014 NFL season").[[136]](#cite_note-149)
* August 16: INVESCO Field at Mile High, the home of the [Denver Broncos](/wiki/Denver_Broncos "Denver Broncos"), was renamed [Sports Authority Field at Mile High](/wiki/Sports_Authority_Field_at_Mile_High "Sports Authority Field at Mile High"). [Invesco Ltd.](/wiki/Invesco_Ltd. "Invesco Ltd.") held the original naming rights to the Broncos' home field since it opened in [2001](/wiki/2001_NFL_season "2001 NFL season"), and Invesco's naming rights agreement was set to expire in 2021. [Sports Authority](/wiki/Sports_Authority "Sports Authority"), a [sporting goods](/wiki/Sporting_goods "Sporting goods") [retailer](/wiki/Retailer "Retailer") based in [Englewood, Colorado](/wiki/Englewood,_Colorado "Englewood, Colorado"), took over the naming rights, and agreed to pay [$](/wiki/Dollar "Dollar")6 million per year for the naming rights to the Broncos' home field.[[137]](#cite_note-150)
* August 23: [Life insurance](/wiki/Life_insurance "Life insurance") company [MetLife](/wiki/MetLife "MetLife") purchased the naming rights to the New Meadowlands Stadium, the new home field of the [New York Jets](/wiki/New_York_Jets "New York Jets") and [New York Giants](/wiki/New_York_Giants "New York Giants") that opened in [2010](/wiki/2010_NFL_season "2010 NFL season"), renaming it [MetLife Stadium](/wiki/MetLife_Stadium "MetLife Stadium"). The life insurance company signed a 25-year, $17 million per year agreement with the Jets and Giants for the stadium's naming rights.[[138]](#cite_note-151)
* October 4: [German](/wiki/Germany "Germany") automaker [Mercedes-Benz](/wiki/Mercedes-Benz "Mercedes-Benz") purchased the naming rights to the Louisiana Superdome, home of the New Orleans Saints. The Saints' home field was officially renamed the [Mercedes-Benz Superdome](/wiki/Mercedes-Benz_Superdome "Mercedes-Benz Superdome") prior to the Saints' Week 7 home game vs. the [Indianapolis Colts](/wiki/Indianapolis_Colts "Indianapolis Colts").[[139]](#cite_note-152)

In addition, the [San Diego Chargers](/wiki/San_Diego_Chargers "San Diego Chargers")' home field, [Qualcomm Stadium](/wiki/Qualcomm_Stadium "Qualcomm Stadium"), was temporarily renamed "Snapdragon Stadium" for a ten-day period from December 16–25, which included the team's Week 15 home game vs. the [Baltimore Ravens](/wiki/Baltimore_Ravens "Baltimore Ravens"), as a marketing tie in for Qualcomm's [Snapdragon](/wiki/Snapdragon_(system_on_chip) "Snapdragon (system on chip)") brand.[[140]](#cite_note-153)

## Uniforms

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2011_NFL_season&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Uniforms")]

This was the last season that [Reebok](/wiki/Reebok "Reebok") exclusively supplied uniforms and sideline caps along with performance and fan apparel for all 32 teams in the league, as [Nike](/wiki/Nike_Inc. "Nike Inc.") and [New Era](/wiki/New_Era_Cap_Company "New Era Cap Company") now have the 40-year rights to manufacture on-field uniforms and fan apparel, with Nike handling uniforms and performance apparel, and New Era with on-field caps. For Reebok, this ends a 10-year exclusivity association that began in [2001](/wiki/2001_NFL_season "2001 NFL season").[[141]](#cite_note-154)

The first Sunday of the season fell on the tenth anniversary of the [September 11 attacks](/wiki/September_11_attacks "September 11 attacks"). To commemorate that event players, coaches, game officials and sideline personnel all wore a special stars and stripes ribbon bearing the dates "9/11/01" and "9/11/11" as a patch or pin. Players were also allowed to wear special red, white and blue gloves and shoes.[[142]](#cite_note-155)

The [Buffalo Bills](/wiki/Buffalo_Bills "Buffalo Bills") introduced redesigned uniforms on June 24, 2011. Early rumors fueled by a *[Madden NFL 12](/wiki/Madden_NFL_12 "Madden NFL 12")* trailer featuring a Bills throwback uniform had indicated the team would be adopting the uniforms the team wore between [1975](/wiki/1975_Buffalo_Bills_season "1975 Buffalo Bills season") and [1983](/wiki/1983_Buffalo_Bills_season "1983 Buffalo Bills season");[[143]](#cite_note-156) the final product indeed resembled those uniforms, with some minor adjustments.[[144]](#cite_note-157) The new uniforms (which marked the first redesign since [2002](/wiki/2002_Buffalo_Bills_season "2002 Buffalo Bills season")) were unveiled at a fan appreciation event at [Ralph Wilson Stadium](/wiki/Ralph_Wilson_Stadium "Ralph Wilson Stadium").[[145]](#cite_note-158) The Bills wore their white "away" uniforms in their week nine home game against the [New York Jets](/wiki/New_York_Jets "New York Jets") as part of a [whiteout](/wiki/Winnipeg_White_Out "Winnipeg White Out") promotion; the last time the team had worn their white uniforms at home was in [1986](/wiki/1986_Buffalo_Bills_season "1986 Buffalo Bills season").[[146]](#cite_note-159)

The [New England Patriots](/wiki/New_England_Patriots "New England Patriots")' uniforms bore a patch bearing the initials "MHK" in honor of team owner [Robert Kraft](/wiki/Robert_Kraft "Robert Kraft")'s wife [Myra Kraft](/wiki/Myra_Kraft "Myra Kraft") who died of cancer in July.[[147]](#cite_note-160) The Patriots wore their red throwback uniforms in their week five game against the New York Jets. They wore their white jerseys at home against the [Dallas Cowboys](/wiki/2011_Dallas_Cowboys_season "2011 Dallas Cowboys season") in week six, thus forcing the Cowboys to use their navy jerseys for the only time all season and the first time since 2009.[[148]](#cite_note-CowboyBlues-161) As per tradition, the Cowboys wore their throwbacks on Thanksgiving Day (November 24) at home against the [Miami Dolphins](/wiki/Miami_Dolphins "Miami Dolphins").[[148]](#cite_note-CowboyBlues-161)

The [St. Louis Rams](/wiki/St._Louis_Rams "St. Louis Rams") wore their throwback uniforms in week 8 against the New Orleans Saints; the date was determined by fan voting.[[149]](#cite_note-162)

The [Baltimore Ravens](/wiki/Baltimore_Ravens "Baltimore Ravens") wore their black alternative jerseys twice in 2011: with black pants against the [Jets](/wiki/2011_New_York_Jets_season "2011 New York Jets season") and with white pants against the [49ers](/wiki/2011_San_Francisco_49ers_season "2011 San Francisco 49ers season").[[150]](#cite_note-163)

The [Tampa Bay Buccaneers](/wiki/Tampa_Bay_Buccaneers "Tampa Bay Buccaneers") wore their orange throwback uniforms during week 13 against Carolina.[[151]](#cite_note-164)

The [Oakland Raiders](/wiki/Oakland_Raiders "Oakland Raiders") wore stickers featuring "AL" on their helmets after owner [Al Davis](/wiki/Al_Davis "Al Davis") died on October 8, 2011.[[152]](#cite_note-165)

This season was the last in which the [Denver Broncos](/wiki/2011_Denver_Broncos_season "2011 Denver Broncos season") wore their navy blue jerseys as their primary jersey, as the team has designated their orange jerseys—the team's alternate home jersey since [2002](/wiki/2002_NFL_season "2002 NFL season")—as their new primary home jersey color, beginning with the [2012](/wiki/2012_NFL_season "2012 NFL season") season. The move was made due to overwhelming fan support to return to using orange as the team's primary home jersey color, which harkens back to the days of the [Orange Crush Defense](/wiki/Orange_Crush_Defense "Orange Crush Defense"), as well as [John Elway](/wiki/John_Elway "John Elway")'s return to the organization as the team's executive vice president of football operations. The team had considered making the switch for the 2011 season, but were too late to notify the NFL of the changes.[[153]](#cite_note-166) The team's navy blue jerseys, which had been their primary home jersey since they were first introduced in [1997](/wiki/1997_NFL_season "1997 NFL season"), will become the alternate jerseys which will be worn in one or two home games each year.[[154]](#cite_note-167)

This season was the last in which the [Seattle Seahawks](/wiki/Seattle_Seahawks "Seattle Seahawks") wore their pacific blue (or "Seahawks blue") jerseys as the team's home jersey, as the team changed their home jersey color to dark navy for the 2012 season.[[155]](#cite_note-168)

## Media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2011_NFL_season&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Media")]

This was the sixth season under the [television contracts](/wiki/NFL_on_television "NFL on television") with the league's television partners: [CBS](/wiki/NFL_on_CBS "NFL on CBS") (all AFC afternoon away games), [Fox](/wiki/NFL_on_Fox "NFL on Fox") (all NFC afternoon away games), [NBC](/wiki/NBC_Sunday_Night_Football "NBC Sunday Night Football") (17 [Sunday Night Football](/wiki/NBC_Sunday_Night_Football "NBC Sunday Night Football") games and the kickoff game), [ESPN](/wiki/ESPN "ESPN") (*[Monday Night Football](/wiki/Monday_Night_Football "Monday Night Football")*), [NFL Network](/wiki/NFL_Network "NFL Network") (eight late-season games on [Thursday night](/wiki/Thursday_Night_Football "Thursday Night Football") and Saturday nights), and [DirecTV](/wiki/DirecTV "DirecTV")'s [NFL Sunday Ticket](/wiki/NFL_Sunday_Ticket "NFL Sunday Ticket") package. These contracts originally ran through at least 2013.

ESPN extended its contract for *Monday Night Football* on September 8, during the opening week of the season. This new contract, valued between $14.2 billion and $15.2 billion, extended ESPN's rights for eight seasons until 2021. It also gave them rights to expanded highlights, international and digital rights, the [Pro Bowl](/wiki/Pro_Bowl "Pro Bowl") beginning with the 2015 installment, and possibly a wild card playoff game.[[156]](#cite_note-169) The league also signed a nine-year extension with CBS, Fox and NBC on their current contracts starting with the 2014 season through 2022.[[157]](#cite_note-170)

CBS added [Marv Albert](/wiki/Marv_Albert "Marv Albert") as a commentator,[[158]](#cite_note-NYT11-171) while [Gus Johnson](/wiki/Gus_Johnson_(sportscaster) "Gus Johnson (sportscaster)") departed from CBS to Fox Sports to call NFL and [college football](/wiki/Fox_College_Football "Fox College Football") games.[[159]](#cite_note-172) ESPN lost both of their sideline reporters from 2010: [Michele Tafoya](/wiki/Michele_Tafoya "Michele Tafoya") moved to NBC, where she replaced the departing [Andrea Kremer](/wiki/Andrea_Kremer "Andrea Kremer"),[[160]](#cite_note-TVbyNumbers-173) and [Suzy Kolber](/wiki/Suzy_Kolber "Suzy Kolber") reduced her on-field work to focus on hosting studio programming. ESPN, who had reduced the roles of its sideline reporters in recent years in response to NFL rule changes, used only one sideline reporter for each game of the 2011 season; among the rotating reporters include Kolber, [Wendi Nix](/wiki/Wendi_Nix "Wendi Nix"), [Ed Werder](/wiki/Ed_Werder "Ed Werder"), [Sal Paolantonio](/wiki/Sal_Paolantonio "Sal Paolantonio"), and [Rachel Nichols](/wiki/Rachel_Nichols_(journalist) "Rachel Nichols (journalist)"). At NFL Network, [Brad Nessler](/wiki/Brad_Nessler "Brad Nessler") and [Mike Mayock](/wiki/Mike_Mayock "Mike Mayock") became its new broadcasting crew, replacing [Bob Papa](/wiki/Bob_Papa "Bob Papa"), [Matt Millen](/wiki/Matt_Millen "Matt Millen"), and [Joe Theismann](/wiki/Joe_Theismann "Joe Theismann").

On December 22, 2010, the league announced that its national radio contract with [Westwood One](/wiki/NFL_on_Westwood_One "NFL on Westwood One"), which was acquired by [Dial Global](/wiki/Dial_Global "Dial Global") in the 2011 offseason, had been extended through 2014.[[161]](#cite_note-174) The league also extended its contract with [Sirius XM Radio](/wiki/Sirius_XM_Radio "Sirius XM Radio") through 2015.[[162]](#cite_note-Sirius_XM_Press_Release-175) In addition to these contracts, and in a first for an NFL team, the [Dallas Cowboys](/wiki/Dallas_Cowboys "Dallas Cowboys") signed a deal to allow for nationwide broadcasts of all of its home and away games broadcast on [Compass Media Networks](/wiki/Compass_Media_Networks "Compass Media Networks"), in addition to its [existing local radio network](/wiki/Dallas_Cowboys_Radio_Network "Dallas Cowboys Radio Network"). Compass also acquired exclusive national broadcast rights to both the International Series and Toronto Series contests.[[163]](#cite_note-176)

The league did not announce plans to compensate their media partners had the season been shortened or canceled as a result of the work stoppage. NBC had ordered several low-cost reality television shows for the [2011–12 TV season](/wiki/2011%E2%80%9312_United_States_network_television_schedule "2011–12 United States network television schedule") in the event that Sunday Night Football could not be played, but other networks had not made public any contingency plans in the event NFL games could not be televised (in the case of CBS and Fox, the Sunday afternoon time slots could have been left unfilled and turned over to the affiliates, likely to be used for [time buys](/wiki/Brokered_programming "Brokered programming") by minor and [extreme sports](/wiki/Extreme_sport "Extreme sport") organizations, or locally programmed [infomercials](/wiki/Infomercial "Infomercial") or movies as they are during the offseason). A work stoppage could have potentially cost these networks billions of dollars in ad revenue and other entertainment platforms that depend on the games being played. (Under the NFL's television contracts, the networks must still pay the league a rights fee regardless of whether or not the league plays any games; a March 2 ruling states that this money must be put into [escrow](/wiki/Escrow "Escrow") and not be spent.)[[164]](#cite_note-177) Meanwhile, the [United Football League](/wiki/United_Football_League_(2009) "United Football League (2009)") had set aside a portion of their television contract for their [2011 UFL season](/wiki/2011_UFL_season "2011 UFL season"), as a potential package of replacement programs for the networks;[[165]](#cite_note-178) while CBS and Fox briefly negotiated with the UFL regarding the package, neither network committed to carrying the games, forcing the UFL to postpone its season by a month.
